headline: High-speed car chase involving federal agent ended with multi-car crash at Nina’s in St. Paul
summary: A high-speed federal immigration enforcement chase in St. Paul caused a multi-car crash, prompting Mayor Kaohly Her to demand an immediate end to Operation Metro Surge; the incident follows a Minneapolis case where ICE agents shot a man due to mistaken identity, intensifying concerns about dangerous pursuit tactics and local police cooperation with federal immigration authorities.

headline: Morrisey names charter school advocate James Paul to West Virginia school board
summary: Gov. Patrick Morrisey appointed charter school advocate Dr. James Paul to the West Virginia Board of Education, replacing a vocal opponent of school choice and signaling a policy shift toward expanding parental access to both public and non-public school options. Paul will prioritize financial accountability amid district crises, including Hancock County's $5 million shortfall and state takeovers of financially troubled systems. He pledges to focus on academic rigor, simple rulemaking, and taxpayer fund oversight while ensuring families can fully utilize available school choices. The governor concurrently pushes for legislative control over the unelected board, calling for systemic reform to improve the state's low education outcomes. The appointment establishes school choice and financial oversight as central pillars of the administration's K-12 education agenda.

headline: $132M in education funding considered by WV Senate committee as schools in financial crisis
summary: Senate Education Chair Amy Grady sponsored two bills to overhaul West Virginia's school funding formula at a combined cost of $132.5 million, addressing critical shortfalls including an $8 million annual special education deficit in Cabell County alone by increasing special education funding and boosting professional staff ratios statewide; both measures advanced from the Senate Education Committee to the Senate Finance Committee for fiscal review.

headline: DeSantis administration publishes proposed AIDS rules, halts state administrative court challenge
summary: The DeSantis administration proposed rule changes to Florida's AIDS Drug Assistance Program that would slash income eligibility from 400% to 130% of the federal poverty level, cutting off HIV medication access for an estimated 16,000 Floridians. The changes would also limit available medications, including eliminating Biktarvy, to address a projected $120 million shortfall. The AIDS Healthcare Foundation challenged the proposal in administrative court, securing a Feb. 18 hearing and forcing a 21-day public comment period. The Department of Health filed notice that proceedings are automatically stayed during rulemaking, while a separate lawsuit seeking transparency on the budget shortfall remains pending in Leon County Circuit Court.

headline: Healey budget boosts court funding after year of uncertainty
summary: Gov. Maura Healey's $62.8 billion state budget proposes $1.48 billion for the judiciary, including a 3% increase for the Trial Court to $999.87 million and a $52 million boost for the Committee for Public Counsel Services, addressing prior warnings about staffing cuts and public defender shortages. The proposal allocates $384.2 million for Trial Court administrative staff, up $24.1 million, amid concerns over federal funding uncertainty and a 30,000-case filing increase. Judicial leaders previously cautioned that inadequate funding would force hundreds of position reductions, while a pay dispute among contracted defense attorneys disrupted court operations. The budget follows a July 2025 supplemental appropriation of $259 million that funded 320 new public defenders and raised private bar counsel rates. The Legislature will conduct hearings through early spring to review the proposal, which aims to stabilize court operations while navigating fiscal constraints and growing caseloads.

headline: South Carolina faces another dangerous RSV season
summary: South Carolina faces concurrent public health crises with a measles outbreak affecting over 900 people (mostly children) and rising RSV infections that previously exceeded 12% test positivity per CDC data. FDA-approved RSV immunizations have proven effective at reducing infant hospitalizations, prompting HHS to issue guidance recommending infants born during RSV season (October-March) receive vaccination within one week of birth, with high-risk adults over 50 also targeted. A family physician author advocates for universal adherence to these immunization policies as the primary defense against both diseases. The article frames vaccination as both a personal responsibility and a public health imperative to prevent emergency room surges. Proactive implementation of these government recommendations is presented as essential to protecting vulnerable populations and maintaining healthcare system capacity.

headline: Chip Roy, Mayes Middleton leading Texas attorney general GOP primary in new poll
summary: A Republican primary debate for Texas attorney general between U.S. Rep. Chip Roy and Aaron Reitz may generate public opinion polling to assess voter sentiment on candidate performance and policy positions, with results potentially influencing campaign strategies and voter perceptions in the lead-up to the election.

headline: Bill to ban ICE agents from wearing masks to get new hearing in NJ Legislature
summary: New Jersey's legislature is considering a bill that would prohibit immigration agents from wearing masks and require officers to display agency insignia, present department-issued identification, or verbally disclose their full name and agency before detaining or arresting individuals. Governor Mikie Sherrill supports the measure, which includes exemptions for undercover operations, disease prevention, and emergencies, while the Trump administration has stated it will not comply, citing officer safety concerns. The bill comes amid nationwide opposition to masked ICE agents and follows a California federal court ruling that struck down a similar state law for targeting only federal agents, unlike New Jersey's version which applies to all law enforcement. Republican state Sen. Jon Bramnick plans to cosponsor the bill despite GOP opposition, while Assemblyman Alex Sauickie criticized it as "performative theater." The Assembly Public Safety and Preparedness Committee will also consider two related immigration bills—codifying the Immigrant Trust Directive and restricting collection of immigration status information—that were vetoed by former Governor Phil Murphy last month.

headline: Idaho Tax Commission anticipates long delays in issuing tax refunds if budget cuts go into effect
summary: Idaho's Joint Finance-Appropriation Committee approved an additional 1% budget cut ($131 million) for most state agencies for FY2026, building on prior 3% reductions to address a projected shortfall; these cuts will delay tax refunds by 12-18 weeks, triggering at least $3.5 million in state interest payments, while a pending tax conformity bill would further reduce state revenue by $155 million this fiscal year.

headline: Idaho Gov. Little’s budget director asks JFAC to restore funding for some services that were cut
summary: The Idaho Legislature's budget committee approved additional 2% across-the-board cuts for FY 2027, bringing total reductions to 5% and slashing $143 million from the general fund despite the governor's budget chief warning of "long-term structural harm" to critical programs including Meals on Wheels for 31,000 seniors, 164 rural career technical education programs, and court services that would increase crime and prison costs. Governor Brad Little's alternative plan would avoid broad cuts by using one-time funding transfers and targeted reductions to restore $13 million in essential services while still projecting a $184 million surplus, leveraging Idaho's strong economy to protect long-term growth and prosperity.

headline: Confronting changing federal guidelines, proposals aim to expand Mainers’ access to vaccines
summary: Maine lawmakers introduced two bills to protect vaccine access by decoupling state policy from federal guidance and expanding pharmacist authority to administer vaccines without doctor notification, citing the ongoing measles outbreak affecting 22 states and federal cuts to children's vaccine recommendations as public health threats. The legislation would accept recommendations from medical organizations like the American Academy of Pediatrics instead of the U.S. CDC and includes liability protections for pharmacists. No opposition was voiced at the hearing.

headline: Cannabis hyperemesis syndrome cases surge in Virginia ERs and more headlines
summary: Cannabis hyperemesis syndrome cases are surging in Virginia emergency rooms, signaling a public health trend that impacts healthcare delivery. This increase in ER visits may strain hospital resources and necessitate enhanced provider training to identify and treat the condition. The development could inform state discussions on cannabis regulation and public health education campaigns.

headline: On the streets of Minneapolis, protesters call out ICE forces. Cellphone videos are a powerful ally.
summary: Federal immigration agents have conducted aggressive enforcement operations in Minneapolis since July 2025, including at least 17 open-fire incidents and the January 7, 2026 killing of Renee Good by ICE officer Jonathan Ross. Agents have entered schools, hospitals, daycares, and churches, detained a 5-year-old child, and shot nurse Alex Pretti while he filmed them. Citizen cellphone videos have documented these actions, contradicting official federal narratives and sparking nationwide demonstrations. The Department of Homeland Security has subpoenaed Minnesota's governor, attorney general, and Minneapolis mayor for allegedly interfering with federal authorities. The operations have been characterized as political retaliation against Minnesota for not voting for Donald Trump, raising significant policy concerns about federal overreach and accountability.

headline: CoreCivic appeals to overturn injunction keeping it from housing ICE detainees
summary: CoreCivic appealed a Leavenworth County injunction blocking it from housing ICE detainees while seeking a city special use permit to be decided March 10 after a Feb. 24 public hearing. City attorneys argued past failures to report crimes and sewage issues constitute "irreparable harm," while CoreCivic seeks reversal to claim damages for an improperly granted injunction.

headline: Campaign to end Ohio property taxes brushes off funding concerns as DeWine warns of 20% sales tax
summary: A proposed Ohio constitutional amendment to abolish property taxes would create a $20 billion state budget shortfall, requiring sales tax rates to potentially reach 20% to fund schools and local services, while Republican leaders oppose income tax increases and amendment supporters argue for unspecified government spending cuts that analysts call unrealistic.

headline: A choice is hidden in the pages of Kentucky’s next state budget
summary: Kentucky's proposed "bare bones" budget makes deep cuts to Medicaid ($800 million shortfall), K-12 education ($100 million cut), and higher education despite having over $1 billion in unspent revenue and a $3.7 billion reserve fund. The budget spends $1.1 billion less than projected revenues to trigger further state income tax cuts, which have already reduced the top rate from 5% to 3.5% at an annual cost of $2.1 billion. These cuts put 35 rural hospitals at risk and continue a 20-year disinvestment in public schools. Only 9% of Kentuckians feel they benefit from the tax reductions, while 72% want lawmakers to prioritize schools, healthcare, and cost-of-living improvements instead. The budget debate represents a fundamental policy choice between continuing tax cuts for the wealthy versus funding essential public services.

headline: Maui Cops Could Still Aid Feds Despite Council Immigration Concerns
summary: Activists claim a local council can exit an FBI agreement, potentially impacting immigration enforcement if the pact involves local cooperation with federal detention or deportation efforts.

headline: Trump agrees not to withhold school funding over DEI
summary: Attorneys General from 19 states, including Nevada, secured an agreement with the Trump administration to abandon its threat to withhold federal K-12 funding from schools maintaining diversity, equity, inclusion, and accessibility initiatives. The U.S. Department of Education had mandated states adopt its Title VI interpretation or risk losing funds for low-income students, English learners, and special education students. Nevada and other states filed lawsuits alleging violations of the Spending Clause, Administrative Procedure Act, and separation of powers. A separate American Federation of Teachers lawsuit resulted in a court vacating the requirement, with the administration's appeal dismissed on February 6. The agreement ensures continued federal funding for vulnerable student populations while preserving schools' lawful DEI efforts.

headline: Federal judge sides with McCook migrant detainee in ACLU Nebraska lawsuit
summary: A federal judge ordered ICE to provide a bond hearing within seven days for a Guatemalan man detained at the McCook facility or release him. The ruling challenges the Trump administration's policy denying bond hearings to most detained immigrants. This policy had already been vacated by a class action lawsuit, but federal officials continued enforcing it. ACLU Nebraska filed the lawsuit on behalf of the man, who has lived in the U.S. for over 20 years. The decision affirms that long-term residents detained within the country are entitled to bond hearings under statutory detention frameworks.

headline: $18M state guarantee for Roger Williams, Fatima hospitals sale heads to McKee’s desk
summary: The Rhode Island General Assembly unanimously approved $18 million in state reserves to facilitate the nonprofit sale of Roger Williams Medical Center and Our Lady of Fatima Hospital, with leaders explicitly calling the intervention critical for public health given the hospitals' combined 500+ beds, 55,000 annual ER visits, and service to over 300,000 patients. The funding prevents imminent closure or receivership of the bankrupt facilities, preserving 2,500 jobs while assuring private investors in $87 million bond financing. The transaction includes 85+ regulatory conditions to ensure operational stability, and returning the hospitals to nonprofit status is expected to generate cost savings and expand behavioral care and cancer services. Lawmakers are also advancing contingency legislation to streamline alternative buyer applications should the Feb. 27 deadline be missed.

headline: Iowa Senate passes 1.75% increase in per-pupil funding for schools
summary: The Iowa Senate passed Senate File 2201 with a 28-20 vote, establishing a 1.75% State Supplemental Aid increase for K-12 schools—below the governor's 2% proposal—raising per-pupil funding to $8,133. Democrats and education advocates warn the insufficient increase will force staff reductions and program cuts, citing Boone's elimination of its orchestra program. The bill includes $47.7 million in one-time funding to prevent property tax increases for districts on budget guarantee. The vote revealed Republican divisions, with three GOP senators joining Democrats in opposition. The measure now moves to the House, where Republicans are considering a higher 2%+ rate, setting up potential intra-party negotiations.

headline: Sen. Dafna Michaelson Jenet to step down from Colorado Legislature
summary: Sen. Dafna Michaelson Jenet's move to direct the David and Laura Merage Foundation for Confronting Antisemitism connects to protests and civil unrest, as antisemitic incidents frequently generate community demonstrations. Her resignation from Senate leadership may impact legislative responses to protest policies and civil disorder management. The foundation addresses public tensions that can escalate into rallies or civil disobedience. Democrats will fill the vacancy through a committee process, potentially affecting future protest regulation policy. Her prior work on social legislation could influence Colorado's approach to community unrest.

headline: Immigration court blocks deportation of Turkish student who was detained in Vermont
summary: A federal immigration judge blocked the Trump administration's deportation proceedings against Turkish doctoral student Rümeysa Öztürk, ruling that the Department of Homeland Security failed to prove she should be removed. The case stems from her March 2024 detention by ICE agents in Vermont after the administration revoked her student visa, allegedly in retaliation for a Gaza war op-ed. Despite her release on bail ordered by a Vermont district judge last May, the administration maintains it may appeal the Jan. 29 ruling, with a DHS spokesperson calling the decision "judicial activism." The administration is separately appealing the district court's bail release order, with a federal appeals panel hearing arguments in September and a decision pending. Öztürk's legal team continues to challenge her arrest on First Amendment grounds in U.S. District Court while warning she could face re-detention if the government appeals.

headline: Measles detected in Washington County wastewater
summary: Vermont health officials detected measles in Washington County wastewater, triggering alerts to healthcare providers and increased statewide testing that reveals the state's 94% vaccination rate falls short of the 95% herd immunity threshold needed for the highly contagious disease. The detection—marking the first positive result from the state's summer-launched surveillance system—highlights concerns about rising national vaccine skepticism and federal policy shifts under HHS Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr., though CMS head Mehmet Oz recently urged vaccination, and validates wastewater monitoring as an effective early warning tool.

headline: Senate GOP pitches deep cuts to CT tax and electric bills
summary: Connecticut Senate Republicans propose a $1.5 billion annual tax relief plan that redirects budget cap savings—previously allocated to pension debt and reserves—into permanent income and payroll tax cuts, eliminating nearly all projected surpluses through 2027 and jeopardizing a child care expansion program. State analysts forecast that future cap revenues will total only $1.2 billion in 2026-27 and $855 million the following year, insufficient to sustain the plan's cost. This marks a dramatic reversal from the caucus's decade-long support for aggressive savings policies that built Connecticut's $4.3 billion rainy day fund. The proposal stands in stark contrast to Governor Lamont's more fiscally conservative $500 million one-time rebate plan. The plan's sustainability is further questioned given the state's $33 billion in remaining unfunded pension obligations.

headline: Report: One ICE facility to expand, two more to be established in Michigan
summary: ICE is expanding its Michigan presence through three facilities: expanding its Detroit Rosa Parks Federal Building office and establishing new locations in Grand Rapids' Waters Center and Southfield's One Towne Square. This move is part of a nationwide ICE expansion and hiring surge aimed at enhancing immigration enforcement, detention, and removal operations. Michigan already houses the North Lake Processing Center in Baldwin, the largest ICE detention facility in the Midwest. The expansion has drawn protests, highlighting political opposition to intensified federal immigration enforcement. These developments signal strengthened federal detention and deportation capacity in the state.
